content.full_text:
State of California                                          Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To    :  Michael Bennett, Executive Director          Date  :  September 8, 1978
        Fair Political Practices Commission
        1100 K Street                                 File No.:
        Sacramento, CA 95814
                                                      Telephone: ATSS (    )
                                                                 (    )

From  :  Robert C. Summers

Subject:  Political Reform Audit Evaluation

        The following suggestions for improvement were received as part of the responses
        to our requests for evaluation of the Political Reform Audit Program. The
        comments are as written by the filer:

1.  I have become an independent contractor this year and would have
    appreciated consultation from the FPPC on setting up a record-
    keeping system.

2.  Improve the forms so that you could balance it against our actual
    bank balance. Our actual bank balance was always correct, but
    it would not balance with the tally sheet of the reports. I
    think a simpler less complex report could be designed, and yet
    not hurt the security that is needed. This is a good program
    but remember the fastest way between two spots is a straight line.

3.  Since the same report is submitted to the Federal Election
    Commission, we suggest that a copy of the federal report be
    submitted to the State in place of having to prepare another report.

4.  A calendar should be made at the beginning of every year and sent
    out to the committees. In the past, we were not aware when the
    reports should have gone out.

5.  It would be helpful to have more detailed seminars on the reporting
    requirements and a periodic bulletin on changes. Most treasurers,
    like myself, are volunteers and don't have the time to keep up on
    our own initiative.

6.  Suggest dropping "Deposits to Account" as is just a repeat of
    payments received; suggest quarterly reports rather than monthly.
    Intent of law would be served and it would save us hours of clerical
    work.

7.  The law is so complex; it discourages many qualified and well
    intended people from serving on committees.

Michael Bennett
September 8, 1978
Page 2


We have sent 800 evaluation forms with our audit reports and received over
400 responses. The form asked for comments regarding (a) attitude and
conduct of the auditors; (b) observations on how the audit was conducted,
and (c) suggestions for improvement. Approximately 90 percent of the
responses rated Question 1 "excellent". Question 2 received only 12 critical
responses and several good suggestions were made in response to Question 3.

We are terminating the evaluation project as of this date.


[signature]

Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

cc: Martin Huff

State of California                                                    90001

* Memorandum                                                    Franchise Tax Board

                                                                FPPC
To     :   Michael Bennett, Executive Director             APR Date 4 10:41[illegible], 1978
           Fair Political Practices Commission
           1100 K Street                                   File No.:
           Sacramento, CA 95814


From  :   Robert C. Summers

Subject:  Political Reform Audit Evaluation


       The Franchise Tax Board has been sending requests for evaluation of the
Political Reform Audit Program with each audit report sent to filers since
early February, 1978.

       Several of the voluntary responses have included the following suggestions
for program improvement which I thought might interest you.

       1.  Consideration should be given to amending the Political Reform
           Act to provide that lobbyists who did not "entertain" State
           officials or enter into "exchanges" with them could satisfy the
           law by filing sworn affidavits to that effect, in addition to
           their monthly reports.  In such cases, the law could then provide
           that audits would only be conducted on a random basis.

       2.  Start audit as soon as possible after the end of campaign while all
           responsible personnel can still be reached and information is
           fresh in their minds. Keep the same auditor whenever possible.
           Establish criteria for selection of committees to be given complete
           audit.  Regulations assigning all of the responsibility to the
           Treasurer need to be re-written.  Responsibility can be divided
           among all key personnel.

       3.  If a lobbyist has two or three good audits, then switch to a
           random audit every two or three years.  It would save a lot of
           time and money and is not necessary.

       4.  Once a filer has established a record of compliance, random audits
           would, in my opinion, be adequate.  If yearly audits are to
           continue, the present system is satisfactory.  The present system
           is much improved.

       5.  I think the instruction booklet in regard to mass mailings is
           ambiguous.  I didn't think it covered dinner invitations.  I
           have no dissatisfaction with your audit program.  This committee
           amended its records to include the $500 unreported.  This
           committee is now closed.  I appreciate the suggestions your auditor
           made.

Michael Bennett
April 28, 1978
Page 2


6. Reduce the amount of expenditures and donations to $200 that
   must be detailed. The purpose of the Act is to find where
   influence occurs. It seems that very little influence, if any,
   could be felt with less than $200 in transactions, particularly
   in light of inflation.

7. Require that photostats of checks be kept and eliminate the
   letter to contributors. The current procedure frightens the
   contributor and has an adverse effect on future contributions.

I will continue to send you these items as they are identified.


Robert C. Lum

Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

cc: Martin Huff

sections.question_synthetic: This document is not a legal question but rather a transmittal of filer feedback and evaluation results regarding the Political Reform Audit Program administered by the Franchise Tax Board.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is correspondence/internal memoranda, not an FPPC advice letter. Contains filer feedback and suggestions for audit program improvements. No traditional Q&A sections. Two related memos dated April 28, 1978 and September 8, 1978. Document appears to be an FTB evaluation project transmittal rather than an FPPC response to a legal question.

embedding.summary: Two memoranda from Robert C. Summers (Franchise Tax Board) to Michael Bennett (FPPC Executive Director) transmitting feedback from filers regarding the Political Reform Audit Program, including suggestions for improvements to reporting forms, audit procedures, and legislative amendments.
